title: Discover Your Linux Distribution
description: Learn how to identify which Linux distribution and kernel version you are running. These are the first commands every Linux user should know.

steps.instruction: Display the name and version of your Linux distribution by reading the os-release file. This file is present on virtually all modern Linux systems.
steps.expectedOutput:
NAME="Ubuntu"
VERSION="22.04.3 LTS (Jammy Jellyfish)"
ID=ubuntu
ID_LIKE=debian
PRETTY_NAME="Ubuntu 22.04.3 LTS"
...

steps.instruction: Display the Linux kernel version currently running. The kernel is the core of the operating system that talks directly to your hardware.
steps.expectedOutput: 5.15.0-88-generic

steps.instruction: Show all system information at once — kernel name, hostname, kernel version, machine architecture, and OS.
steps.expectedOutput: Linux myhost 5.15.0-88-generic #98-Ubuntu SMP Mon Oct 2 15:18:56 UTC 2023 x86_64 x86_64 x86_64 GNU/Linux

steps.instruction: Check how long the system has been running since its last reboot using the uptime command.
Display the hostname of your machine. The hostname identifies your computer on a network.
steps.expectedOutput: myhost

title: Explore the Shell Environment
description: Get comfortable with the shell — learn who you are logged in as, which shell you are using, and how to find where programs live.

steps.instruction: Find out which user you are currently logged in as. This is one of the most fundamental commands in Linux.
steps.expectedOutput: student

steps.instruction: Display the shell you are currently using. The SHELL environment variable stores the path to your default shell.
steps.expectedOutput: /bin/bash

steps.instruction: List all environment variables currently set in your session. Environment variables control how programs behave.
steps.expectedOutput:
SHELL=/bin/bash
PWD=/home/student
LOGNAME=student
HOME=/home/student
LANG=en_US.UTF-8
USER=student
PATH=/usr/local/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/sbin:/usr/bin:/sbin:/bin
...

steps.instruction: Find where the 'ls' command binary is located on disk using the 'which' command.
steps.expectedOutput: /usr/bin/ls

steps.instruction: Display the value of the PATH variable. PATH tells the shell where to look for executable programs.
steps.expectedOutput: /usr/local/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/sbin:/usr/bin:/sbin:/bin

title: Getting Help in Linux
description: Learn how to use the built-in help systems. Knowing how to find answers yourself is the most important Linux skill.

steps.instruction: Open the manual page for the 'ls' command. Man pages are the built-in documentation for almost every command. Press 'q' to quit.
steps.expectedOutput:
LS(1)                            User Commands                           LS(1)

NAME
       ls - list directory contents

SYNOPSIS
       ls [OPTION]... [FILE]...

DESCRIPTION
       List  information  about the FILEs (the current directory by default).
...

steps.instruction: Use the --help flag to get a quick summary of the 'cp' command options. Most commands support --help.
steps.expectedOutput:
Usage: cp [OPTION]... [-T] SOURCE DEST
  or:  cp [OPTION]... SOURCE... DIRECTORY
  or:  cp [OPTION]... -t DIRECTORY SOURCE...
Copy SOURCE to DEST, or multiple SOURCE(s) to DIRECTORY.
...

steps.instruction: Use the 'type' command to find out whether a command is a shell built-in, an alias, or an external program.
steps.expectedOutput: cd is a shell builtin

steps.instruction: Search the man page database for commands related to 'password'. The -k flag searches short descriptions.
steps.expectedOutput:
chpasswd (8)         - update passwords in batch mode
passwd (1)           - change user password
passwd (5)           - the password file
...

title: Moving Around the Filesystem
description: Practice the essential navigation commands: pwd, cd, and ls. These three commands will be used in almost every terminal session.

/home/student

steps.instruction: Use a relative path to go up one directory from your home, then list what is there.
steps.expectedOutput:
/home
student

title: Creating and Removing Directories
description: Learn to create directory structures and clean them up. The mkdir and rmdir commands are your tools for organising files.

steps.instruction: Create a new directory called 'lab-practice' in your home directory.
steps.expectedOutput: /home/student/lab-practice

steps.instruction: Create a nested directory structure in one command using the -p flag. This creates parent directories as needed.
steps.expectedOutput:
/home/student/lab-practice:
projects

/home/student/lab-practice/projects:
webapp

/home/student/lab-practice/projects/webapp:
src

steps.instruction: Create multiple directories at the same level in one command.
steps.expectedOutput: config  docs  projects  tests

steps.instruction: Remove the empty 'tests' directory using rmdir. Note: rmdir only works on empty directories.
steps.expectedOutput: config  docs  projects

steps.instruction: Clean up the entire lab-practice directory tree using rm -r. Be careful with this command — it deletes recursively!
steps.expectedOutput: ls: cannot access '/home/student/lab-practice': No such file or directory

title: Copying, Moving, and Renaming Files
description: Practice copying and moving files and directories. In Linux, renaming is done with the 'mv' command.

steps.instruction: Create a test file, then copy it to a new name. The cp command duplicates files.
steps.expectedOutput: Hello Linux

steps.instruction: Rename the backup file using mv. In Linux, 'mv' is used for both moving and renaming.
steps.expectedOutput: /tmp/original.txt  /tmp/renamed.txt

steps.instruction: Copy an entire directory recursively using cp -r.
steps.expectedOutput:
/tmp/destination:
file.txt  subdir

/tmp/destination/subdir:

steps.instruction: Move a file into a directory. When the target is a directory, mv places the file inside it.
steps.expectedOutput: file.txt  renamed.txt  subdir

steps.instruction: Clean up all test files.
steps.expectedOutput: Cleanup complete

title: Reading File Permissions
description: Learn to read and understand the permission string shown by 'ls -l'. Every file has permissions for the owner, group, and others.

steps.instruction: Create a test file and view its default permissions. The permission string has 10 characters: file type + 3 sets of rwx.
steps.expectedOutput: -rw-r--r-- 1 student student 0 Oct 10 14:30 /tmp/permtest.txt

steps.instruction: Use stat to see permissions in both symbolic and numeric (octal) format.
steps.expectedOutput: Access: (0644/-rw-r--r--)  Uid: ( 1000/ student)   Gid: ( 1000/ student)

steps.instruction: Check permissions on a directory. Notice the 'd' at the start and the 'x' bits — execute on a directory means you can enter it.
steps.expectedOutput: drwxrwxrwt 12 root root 4096 Oct 10 14:30 /tmp

steps.instruction: Compare permissions of different system files to see how Linux protects sensitive data.
steps.expectedOutput:
-rw-r--r-- 1 root root   1832 Oct  5 12:00 /etc/passwd
-rw-r----- 1 root shadow  987 Oct  5 12:00 /etc/shadow

title: Changing Permissions with chmod
description: Practice changing file permissions using both symbolic (u+x) and numeric (755) notation.

steps.instruction: Make a file executable for the owner using symbolic notation. The 'u' means user (owner), '+x' adds execute.
steps.command:
echo '#!/bin/bash
echo Hello' > /tmp/myscript.sh && chmod u+x /tmp/myscript.sh && ls -l /tmp/myscript.sh
steps.expectedOutput: -rwxr--r-- 1 student student 28 Oct 10 14:35 /tmp/myscript.sh

steps.instruction: Set permissions using numeric (octal) notation. 755 means rwx for owner, r-x for group and others.
steps.expectedOutput: -rwxr-xr-x 1 student student 28 Oct 10 14:35 /tmp/myscript.sh

steps.instruction: Remove all permissions for 'others' (everyone who is not the owner or in the group).
steps.expectedOutput: -rwxr-x--- 1 student student 28 Oct 10 14:35 /tmp/myscript.sh

steps.instruction: Set the file to read-only for everyone using numeric notation. 444 means r--r--r--.
steps.expectedOutput: -r--r--r-- 1 student student 28 Oct 10 14:35 /tmp/myscript.sh

steps.instruction: Clean up the test file.
steps.expectedOutput: Done

title: Changing Ownership with chown
description: Learn to change file ownership. Ownership determines which permission set (user, group, others) applies to each person.

steps.instruction: Create a file and check its current ownership. New files are owned by the user who creates them.
steps.expectedOutput: -rw-r--r-- 1 student student 0 Oct 10 14:40 /tmp/owntest.txt

steps.instruction: Change the group ownership of the file. You can change group to any group you belong to without sudo.
steps.expectedOutput: -rw-r--r-- 1 student student 0 Oct 10 14:40 /tmp/owntest.txt

steps.instruction: View which groups your user belongs to. You can change file group ownership to any of these groups.
steps.expectedOutput: student adm sudo

steps.instruction: Use chown with the user:group syntax to set both at once (requires sudo for changing user).
steps.expectedOutput: -rw-r--r-- 1 root root 0 Oct 10 14:40 /tmp/owntest.txt

steps.instruction: Clean up.
steps.expectedOutput: Done

title: Special Permissions: SUID, SGID, and Sticky Bit
description: Explore the special permission bits that go beyond basic rwx. These are critical for system security.

steps.instruction: Find the SUID bit on the passwd command. SUID lets a program run as its owner (root), which is why normal users can change their password.
steps.expectedOutput: -rwsr-xr-x 1 root root 59976 Oct  5 12:00 /usr/bin/passwd

steps.instruction: Check the sticky bit on /tmp. The 't' at the end means only a file's owner can delete it, even though everyone can write to /tmp.
steps.expectedOutput: drwxrwxrwt 12 root root 4096 Oct 10 14:45 /tmp

steps.instruction: Find all SUID files on the system. These are security-sensitive because they run with elevated privileges.
steps.expectedOutput:
/usr/bin/passwd
/usr/bin/sudo
/usr/bin/chfn
/usr/bin/chsh
/usr/bin/gpasswd
/usr/bin/newgrp
/usr/bin/mount
/usr/bin/umount
/usr/bin/su
...

steps.instruction: Create a directory with the SGID bit set. New files created inside will inherit the directory's group.
steps.expectedOutput: drwxrwsr-x 2 student student 4096 Oct 10 14:45 /tmp/sgid-test

steps.instruction: Clean up.
steps.expectedOutput: Done

title: Exploring User Accounts
description: Learn how Linux stores user information and how to inspect user accounts on the system.

steps.instruction: Display your user ID (UID), group ID (GID), and all groups you belong to using the 'id' command.
steps.expectedOutput: uid=1000(student) gid=1000(student) groups=1000(student),4(adm),27(sudo)

steps.instruction: Look at the /etc/passwd file to see all user accounts. Each line has 7 colon-separated fields.
steps.expectedOutput:
root:x:0:0:root:/root:/bin/bash
daemon:x:1:1:daemon:/usr/sbin:/usr/sbin/nologin
bin:x:2:2:bin:/bin:/usr/sbin/nologin
sys:x:3:3:sys:/dev:/usr/sbin/nologin
sync:x:4:65534:sync:/bin:/bin/sync

steps.instruction: Find your own entry in /etc/passwd using grep. This shows your UID, home directory, and default shell.
steps.expectedOutput: student:x:1000:1000:Student User:/home/student:/bin/bash

steps.instruction: Count how many user accounts exist on the system. Most are system accounts used by services.
steps.expectedOutput: 35 /etc/passwd

steps.instruction: List only 'real' human users (UID >= 1000) by filtering /etc/passwd.
steps.expectedOutput: student 1000

title: Managing Groups
description: Understand how groups work in Linux and how to view and manage group memberships.

steps.instruction: List all groups on the system by viewing /etc/group. Each line shows group name, password placeholder, GID, and members.
steps.expectedOutput:
root:x:0:
daemon:x:1:
bin:x:2:
sys:x:3:
adm:x:4:syslog,student
tty:x:5:
disk:x:6:
lp:x:7:
...

steps.instruction: Show which groups your user belongs to.
steps.expectedOutput: student : student adm sudo

steps.instruction: Create a new group called 'devteam' using sudo.
steps.expectedOutput: devteam:x:1001:

steps.instruction: Clean up by removing the group.
steps.expectedOutput: Group removed

title: Creating and Deleting Users
description: Practice creating user accounts with different options and then removing them safely.

steps.instruction: Create a new user called 'testuser' with a home directory. The -m flag ensures the home directory is created.
steps.expectedOutput: testuser:x:1001:1001::/home/testuser:/bin/bash

steps.instruction: Verify the home directory was created and check its permissions.
steps.expectedOutput: drwxr-x--- 2 testuser testuser 4096 Oct 10 15:00 /home/testuser

steps.instruction: Set a password for the new user (in a script-friendly way).
steps.expectedOutput: Password set

steps.instruction: Check the user's password entry in /etc/shadow (hashed, not plaintext).
steps.expectedOutput: testuser:$6$randomsalt$hash

steps.instruction: Delete the user and their home directory. The -r flag removes the home directory too.
steps.command: sudo userdel -r testuser && echo 'User deleted' && ls /home/testuser 2>&1
steps.expectedOutput:
User deleted
ls: cannot access '/home/testuser': No such file or directory

title: Creating and Editing Files from the Command Line
description: Learn to create files and add content without opening a text editor. These techniques are essential for scripting.

steps.instruction: Create a new file with content using echo and output redirection (>). The > operator creates or overwrites the file.
steps.command: echo 'First line of my file' > /tmp/myfile.txt && cat /tmp/myfile.txt

EOF
cat /tmp/config.txt

port=8080

title: Sending Signals and Killing Processes
description: Learn to control processes by sending signals — stop, continue, and terminate running programs.

steps.instruction: Start a background process that we can practice with. The & puts it in the background.
steps.command:
sleep 300 &
echo "Started background process with PID: $!"

steps.instruction: Send the SIGSTOP signal to pause the process, then check its status.
steps.expectedOutput: [1]+  5432 Stopped                 sleep 300

steps.instruction: Resume the stopped process with SIGCONT.
steps.expectedOutput: [1]+  5432 Running                 sleep 300 &

steps.instruction: Terminate the process gracefully with SIGTERM (the default signal), then verify it is gone.
steps.expectedOutput: [1]+  5432 Terminated              sleep 300

steps.instruction: Write a script with functions that performs a system health check.
steps.command:
cat << 'SCRIPT' > /tmp/healthcheck.sh
#!/bin/bash
check_disk() {
  local usage=$(df / | awk 'NR==2 {print $5}' | tr -d '%')
  if [ "$usage" -gt 90 ]; then
    echo "WARN: Disk usage at ${usage}%"
    return 1
  fi
  echo "OK: Disk usage at ${usage}%"
  return 0
}

check_memory() {
  local avail=$(awk '/MemAvailable/ {printf "%.0f", $2/1024}' /proc/meminfo)
  echo "OK: ${avail}MB memory available"
  return 0
}

check_load() {
  local load=$(cat /proc/loadavg | cut -d' ' -f1)
  echo "OK: Load average is $load"
  return 0
}

echo "=== System Health Check ==="
check_disk
check_memory
check_load
echo "=== Check Complete ==="
SCRIPT
chmod +x /tmp/healthcheck.sh && /tmp/healthcheck.sh

OK: 5543MB memory available

#!/bin/bash
validate_port() {
  local port=$1
  if [[ ! "$port" =~ ^[0-9]+$ ]]; then
    echo "Error: '$port' is not a number" >&2
    return 1
  fi
  if [ "$port" -lt 1 ] || [ "$port" -gt 65535 ]; then
    echo "Error: Port must be 1-65535" >&2
    return 1
  fi
  echo "Port $port is valid"
  return 0
}

title: Interview Scenario: Permission Denied Errors
description: A developer reports 'Permission denied' when trying to access a file. Systematically diagnose the cause.

steps.expectedOutput:

steps.instruction: Step 3: Analyse the permission mismatch. The file is owned by root:appgroup with mode 640.
steps.command:
echo '=== Permission Analysis ==='
echo 'File: -rw-r----- root:appgroup'
echo 'User: developer (groups: developer, sudo)'
echo ''
echo 'Owner (root): rw- ✓ can read/write'
echo 'Group (appgroup): r-- ✓ can read'
echo 'Others: --- ✗ no access'
echo ''
echo 'Problem: developer is NOT in appgroup → falls into "others" → denied'
steps.expectedOutput:

File: -rw-r----- root:appgroup
User: developer (groups: developer, sudo)

Owner (root): rw- ✓ can read/write
Group (appgroup): r-- ✓ can read
Others: --- ✗ no access

Problem: developer is NOT in appgroup → falls into "others" → denied

steps.instruction: Step 4: Also check directory permissions — you need execute on every directory in the path.
steps.command:

-rw-r----- root appgroup config.yml'

drwxr-xr-x root root opt
drwxr-x--- root appgroup app
-rw-r----- root appgroup config.yml

steps.instruction: Step 5: Identify TWO permission barriers — both the directory and the file block access.
steps.command:
echo '=== Root Cause ==='
echo '1. /opt/app/ directory is drwxr-x--- (750) owned by root:appgroup'
echo '   → developer cannot even enter the directory'
echo '2. config.yml is -rw-r----- (640) owned by root:appgroup'
echo '   → even if they could enter, they cannot read the file'

Oct 10 16:55:07 myhost myapp[8950]: Error: Cannot bind to port 8080: Address already in use
Oct 10 16:55:07 myhost systemd[1]: myapp.service: Failed with result exit-code.
Oct 10 17:00:00 myhost systemd[1]: myapp.service: Start request repeated too quickly.
Oct 10 17:00:00 myhost systemd[1]: myapp.service: Failed with result exit-code.'

Oct 10 16:55:06 myhost systemd[1]: myapp.service: Scheduled restart job.
Oct 10 16:55:06 myhost systemd[1]: Started My Application Server.
Oct 10 16:55:07 myhost myapp[8950]: Error: Cannot bind to port 8080: Address already in use
Oct 10 16:55:07 myhost systemd[1]: myapp.service: Failed with result exit-code.
Oct 10 17:00:00 myhost systemd[1]: myapp.service: Start request repeated too quickly.
Oct 10 17:00:00 myhost systemd[1]: myapp.service: Failed with result exit-code.

steps.instruction: Step 4: Check the unit file for restart configuration and dependencies.
steps.command:
echo '[Unit]
Description=My Application Server
After=network.target

[Service]
Type=simple
ExecStart=/opt/myapp/bin/server
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=5

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target'
echo ''
echo 'Issue: No dependency on nginx — both try to use port 8080'
steps.expectedOutput:

Description=My Application Server
After=network.target

[Service]
Type=simple
ExecStart=/opt/myapp/bin/server
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=5

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target

Issue: No dependency on nginx — both try to use port 8080

steps.instruction: Step 5: Summarise the diagnosis and provide the fix.
steps.command:
echo '=== Diagnosis ==='
echo 'myapp.service crashes because nginx is already listening on port 8080.'
echo 'The service has Restart=on-failure, so systemd keeps restarting it, but it'
echo 'fails every time because the port conflict persists.'
echo ''
echo '=== Fix Options ==='
echo '1. Change myapp to use a different port (e.g., 8081)'
echo '2. Configure nginx as a reverse proxy and remove direct port binding'
echo '3. Stop nginx if it should not be running: sudo systemctl stop nginx'
echo '4. Add Conflicts=nginx.service to the unit file if they are mutually exclusive'
echo ''
echo '=== After fixing, reset the failed state: ==='
echo 'sudo systemctl reset-failed myapp'
echo 'sudo systemctl start myapp'
steps.expectedOutput:
=== Diagnosis ===
myapp.service crashes because nginx is already listening on port 8080.
The service has Restart=on-failure, so systemd keeps restarting it, but it
fails every time because the port conflict persists.

=== Fix Options ===
1. Change myapp to use a different port (e.g., 8081)
2. Configure nginx as a reverse proxy and remove direct port binding
3. Stop nginx if it should not be running: sudo systemctl stop nginx
4. Add Conflicts=nginx.service to the unit file if they are mutually exclusive

sudo systemctl start myapp

title: Log Investigation
description: Investigate system logs with journalctl, tail, grep, and dmesg. Logs are your primary evidence when diagnosing any issue.

steps.instruction: View the most recent systemd journal entries. The -n flag limits output and -p filters by priority level.
steps.expectedOutput:
Oct 10 14:00:00 myhost systemd[1]: Started Regular background program processing daemon.
Oct 10 14:00:01 myhost CRON[6789]: (root) CMD (test -x /usr/sbin/anacron)
Oct 10 14:05:00 myhost sshd[1200]: Accepted publickey for student from 192.168.1.100 port 54321 ssh2
...

steps.instruction: View logs for a specific service and time range using journalctl filters.
steps.expectedOutput:
Oct 10 13:15:00 myhost sshd[1200]: Accepted publickey for student from 192.168.1.100 port 54321 ssh2
Oct 10 13:15:00 myhost sshd[1200]: pam_unix(sshd:session): session opened for user student
...

steps.instruction: Monitor a log file in real-time using tail -f. This is how you watch for events as they happen. We use timeout to auto-exit.
steps.command: timeout 3 sudo tail -f /var/log/syslog 2>/dev/null | head -5 || echo 'Log monitoring demo complete'
steps.expectedOutput:
Oct 10 14:10:01 myhost CRON[7890]: (root) CMD (test -x /usr/sbin/anacron)
Log monitoring demo complete

steps.instruction: Search authentication logs for failed login attempts using grep. This is a key security investigation technique.
steps.command: sudo grep 'Failed password' /var/log/auth.log 2>/dev/null | tail -5 || echo 'No failed password attempts found'
steps.expectedOutput:
Oct 10 12:30:45 myhost sshd[4567]: Failed password for invalid user admin from 203.0.113.50 port 45678 ssh2
Oct 10 12:31:12 myhost sshd[4568]: Failed password for invalid user test from 203.0.113.50 port 45679 ssh2
...

steps.command: sudo dmesg --level=err,warn | tail -10 || echo 'No kernel errors or warnings found'

[ 1234.567890] EXT4-fs warning (device sda1): ext4_dx_add_entry:2086: Directory inode 12345 index full
...

steps.command: journalctl -b -p err --no-pager | head -10 || echo 'No errors in current boot'
steps.expectedOutput:
Oct 07 12:00:01 myhost kernel: ACPI Error: Namespace lookup failure

title: Slow Server Diagnosis
description: Walk through the systematic workflow for diagnosing a slow server: uptime, top, vmstat, free, and iostat. This is the exact sequence experienced sysadmins follow.

steps.instruction: Step 1: Check uptime and load averages. If the load average exceeds the number of CPU cores, the system is overloaded.
steps.expectedOutput:
14:00:00 up 3 days,  2:15,  1 user,  load average: 0.15, 0.10, 0.05
CPU cores: 4

steps.instruction: Step 2: Run top in batch mode to identify the top CPU and memory consumers. Look at %CPU, %MEM, and the process STATE column.
steps.expectedOutput:
top - 14:00:00 up 3 days,  2:15,  1 user,  load average: 0.15, 0.10, 0.05
Tasks:  92 total,   1 running,  91 sleeping,   0 stopped,   0 zombie
%Cpu(s):  2.3 us,  1.0 sy,  0.0 ni, 96.5 id,  0.1 wa,  0.0 hi,  0.1 si
MiB Mem :   7957.1 total,   2100.3 free,   1456.5 used,   4400.3 buff/cache
MiB Swap:   2048.0 total,   2048.0 free,      0.0 used.   5320.2 avail Mem

    PID USER      PR  NI    VIRT    RES    SHR S  %CPU  %MEM     TIME+ COMMAND
      1 root      20   0  169584  13256   8432 S   0.0   0.2   0:08.12 systemd
...

steps.instruction: Step 3: Use vmstat to check for swapping and I/O wait. Non-zero si/so means the system is swapping — a major performance killer.
Step 4: Check memory in detail with free. Compare 'used' vs 'available' — Linux uses free RAM for cache, so 'free' being low is normal.
steps.expectedOutput:
total        used        free      shared  buff/cache   available
Mem:           7.8Gi       1.4Gi       2.1Gi        12Mi       4.3Gi       5.2Gi
Swap:          2.0Gi          0B       2.0Gi

steps.instruction: Step 5: Check disk I/O with iostat. High await times or high %util indicate a storage bottleneck.
steps.expectedOutput:
Linux 5.15.0-88-generic (myhost)

avg-cpu:  %user   %nice %system %iowait  %steal   %idle
           2.3    0.0     1.0     0.1     0.0    96.5

Device      r/s     rkB/s   w/s     wkB/s  await  %util
sda        1.23    45.6    2.34    89.0    1.23   0.5%

steps.instruction: Step 6: Generate a quick summary report combining all findings into a single diagnostic overview.
steps.command:
echo '=== Slow Server Diagnostic Summary ==='
echo "Load: $(cat /proc/loadavg | cut -d' ' -f1-3) ($(nproc) cores)"
echo "Memory: $(free -h | awk '/Mem:/ {print $3, "used,", $7, "available"}')"
echo "Swap: $(free -h | awk '/Swap:/ {print $3, "used"}')"
echo "Disk: $(df -h / | awk 'NR==2 {print $5, "used"}')"
echo "Zombies: $(ps aux | awk '$8~/Z/' | wc -l)"
echo "IO Wait: $(vmstat 1 2 | tail -1 | awk '{print $16"%"}')"
echo '=== End ==='
steps.expectedOutput:
=== Slow Server Diagnostic Summary ===
Load: 0.15 0.10 0.05 (4 cores)
Memory: 1.4Gi used, 5.2Gi available
Swap: 0B used
Disk: 26% used
Zombies: 0
IO Wait: 0%
=== End ===

title: Disk Full Emergency
description: Respond to a disk full alert: identify the full filesystem, drill down with du, find large files, truncate logs, and reclaim space from deleted files held open by processes.

steps.instruction: Step 1: Identify which filesystem is full or nearly full using df.
steps.expectedOutput:
Filesystem      Size  Used Avail Use% Mounted on
/dev/sda1        50G   12G   35G  26% /
/dev/sda2       200G   45G  145G  24% /home

steps.instruction: Step 2: Drill down into the largest mount point to find which top-level directories are consuming the most space.
steps.expectedOutput:
12G	/
4.5G	/var
3.2G	/usr
2.1G	/home
1.2G	/opt
500M	/tmp
...

steps.instruction: Step 3: Drill deeper into the largest directory to pinpoint the culprit.
steps.expectedOutput:
4.5G	/var
2.1G	/var/log
1.2G	/var/cache
800M	/var/cache/apt
500M	/var/log/app
300M	/var/lib
...

steps.instruction: Step 4: Find the specific large files that can be truncated or deleted.
steps.command: sudo find /var/log -type f -size +50M -exec ls -lh {} \; 2>/dev/null | sort -k5 -rh | head -10
steps.expectedOutput:
-rw-r--r-- 1 root root 1.5G Oct 10 14:00 /var/log/app/application.log
-rw-r----- 1 syslog adm  120M Oct 10 14:00 /var/log/syslog.1
-rw-r----- 1 syslog adm   85M Oct  9 23:59 /var/log/kern.log.1
...

steps.instruction: Step 5: Check for deleted files still held open by processes — these are invisible to du but still consume space.
steps.expectedOutput:
COMMAND   PID   USER   FD   TYPE DEVICE     SIZE/OFF NLINK  NODE NAME
java     2345   app   15w   REG    8,1  5368709120     0 67890 /var/log/app/old.log (deleted)

steps.instruction: Step 6: Show the remediation commands — truncate the large log and reclaim deleted file space by restarting the holding process.
steps.command:
echo '=== Emergency Remediation ==='
echo '1. Truncate oversized log (keeps file handle valid):'
echo '   sudo truncate -s 0 /var/log/app/application.log'
echo '2. Reclaim deleted file space (restart holding process):'
echo '   sudo systemctl restart app'
echo '3. Clean package cache:'
echo '   sudo apt-get clean'
echo '4. Verify space reclaimed:'
echo '   df -h /'
echo '5. Prevent recurrence — set up logrotate'
steps.expectedOutput:
=== Emergency Remediation ===
1. Truncate oversized log (keeps file handle valid):
   sudo truncate -s 0 /var/log/app/application.log
2. Reclaim deleted file space (restart holding process):
   sudo systemctl restart app
3. Clean package cache:
   sudo apt-get clean
4. Verify space reclaimed:
   df -h /
5. Prevent recurrence — set up logrotate

title: SSH Troubleshooting
description: Diagnose SSH connection failures step by step: test network connectivity, check the port, use verbose SSH, and verify the sshd service.

steps.instruction: Step 1: Test basic network connectivity to the target host with ping.
steps.expectedOutput:
PING localhost (127.0.0.1) 56(84) bytes of data.
64 bytes from localhost (127.0.0.1): icmp_seq=1 ttl=64 time=0.023 ms

64 bytes from localhost (127.0.0.1): icmp_seq=3 ttl=64 time=0.028 ms

--- localhost ping statistics ---
3 packets transmitted, 3 received, 0% packet loss

steps.instruction: Step 2: Check if the SSH port (22) is open using nc (netcat). If this fails, the service is down or a firewall is blocking.
steps.command: nc -zv -w3 localhost 22 2>&1 || echo 'Port 22 is NOT reachable'
steps.expectedOutput: Connection to localhost (127.0.0.1) 22 port [tcp/ssh] succeeded!

steps.instruction: Step 3: Attempt an SSH connection with verbose output to see exactly where it fails. The -v flag shows the handshake steps.

steps.expectedOutput:

debug1: Connection established.
debug1: Authentication succeeded (publickey).
...

steps.instruction: Step 4: Check if the sshd service is running and listening on the correct port.
steps.command: systemctl is-active sshd && sudo ss -tlnp | grep sshd

steps.instruction: Step 5: Check the SSH server configuration for common issues like wrong port, disabled password auth, or denied users.
steps.expectedOutput:
port 22
permitroot prohibit-password
pubkeyauthentication yes
passwordauthentication yes
maxauthtries 6

steps.instruction: Step 6: Check auth logs for SSH-related errors to see why specific users are being rejected.
steps.expectedOutput:
Oct 10 14:00:00 myhost sshd[1200]: Accepted publickey for student from 192.168.1.100 port 54321 ssh2
Oct 10 14:00:00 myhost sshd[1200]: pam_unix(sshd:session): session opened for user student
...

title: Service Won't Start
description: Diagnose why a systemd service fails to start: check status, read journal logs, validate configuration, and detect port conflicts.

steps.instruction: Step 1: Check the service status to see the error message and exit code.
steps.expectedOutput:
● cron.service - Regular background program processing daemon
     Loaded: loaded (/lib/systemd/system/cron.service; enabled; vendor preset: enabled)
     Active: active (running) since Mon 2023-10-07 12:00:00 UTC; 3 days ago
       Docs: man:cron(8)
   Main PID: 489 (cron)
      Tasks: 1 (limit: 4915)
     Memory: 1.0M
        CPU: 45ms
     CGroup: /system.slice/cron.service
             └─489 /usr/sbin/cron -f

steps.instruction: Step 2: Read the journal logs for the service to find the actual error message. This is where the root cause usually appears.
steps.expectedOutput:
Oct 07 12:00:00 myhost systemd[1]: Started Regular background program processing daemon.
Oct 10 14:00:01 myhost CRON[6789]: (root) CMD (test -x /usr/sbin/anacron || ( cd / && run-parts --report /etc/cron.hourly ))
...

steps.instruction: Step 3: Validate the service's configuration file for syntax errors. Many services have a config-test mode.
steps.command: sudo sshd -t 2>&1 && echo 'SSH config syntax OK' || echo 'SSH config has errors'
steps.expectedOutput: SSH config syntax OK

steps.instruction: Step 4: Check for port conflicts — another process may already be using the port your service needs.
steps.command: sudo ss -tlnp | grep -E ':22|:80|:443|:8080' || echo 'No conflicts on common ports'

steps.instruction: Step 5: Check if the service's binary exists and is executable, and verify required directories exist.
steps.command: which cron && ls -l $(which cron) && echo '---' && ls -ld /var/spool/cron/ 2>/dev/null || echo '/var/spool/cron not found'
steps.expectedOutput:
/usr/sbin/cron
-rwxr-xr-x 1 root root 43568 Oct  5 12:00 /usr/sbin/cron
---
drwxr-xr-x 2 root root 4096 Oct  5 12:00 /var/spool/cron/

steps.instruction: Step 6: Show the full troubleshooting checklist for a service that won't start.
steps.command:
echo '=== Service Won't Start Checklist ==='
echo '1. systemctl status <service>     → check Active state and exit code'
echo '2. journalctl -u <service> -n 50  → read the actual error message'
echo '3. <service> -t / --check-config   → validate config syntax'
echo '4. ss -tlnp | grep :<port>         → check for port conflicts'
echo '5. ls -l <binary>                  → verify binary exists'
echo '6. systemctl cat <service>         → review unit file for typos'
echo '7. sudo systemctl reset-failed <service> → clear failed state'
echo '8. sudo systemctl restart <service>      → try again'
steps.expectedOutput:
=== Service Won't Start Checklist ===
1. systemctl status <service>     → check Active state and exit code
2. journalctl -u <service> -n 50  → read the actual error message
3. <service> -t / --check-config   → validate config syntax
4. ss -tlnp | grep :<port>         → check for port conflicts
5. ls -l <binary>                  → verify binary exists
6. systemctl cat <service>         → review unit file for typos
7. sudo systemctl reset-failed <service> → clear failed state
8. sudo systemctl restart <service>      → try again

title: Permission Denied Investigation
description: Systematically diagnose 'Permission denied' errors: check file permissions, user identity, parent directory access, and fix with chmod/chown.

steps.instruction: Step 1: Check the file's permissions and ownership with ls -la. Identify who owns it and what access each category has.
steps.expectedOutput: -rw-r----- 1 root shadow 987 Oct  5 12:00 /etc/shadow

steps.instruction: Step 2: Check who you are and what groups you belong to using id. Compare this against the file's owner and group.
steps.expectedOutput: uid=1000(student) gid=1000(student) groups=1000(student),4(adm),27(sudo)

steps.instruction: Step 3: Use stat to see the full permission details including numeric mode, owner, and group.
steps.expectedOutput:
File: /etc/shadow
Access: (0640/-rw-r-----)  Uid: (    0/    root)   Gid: (   42/  shadow)

steps.instruction: Step 4: Check parent directory permissions. You need execute (x) permission on EVERY directory in the path to reach a file.
steps.command:
namei -l /etc/shadow 2>/dev/null || echo 'f: /etc/shadow
drwxr-xr-x root root /
drwxr-xr-x root root etc
-rw-r----- root shadow shadow'
steps.expectedOutput:
f: /etc/shadow
drwxr-xr-x root root /
drwxr-xr-x root root etc
-rw-r----- root shadow shadow

steps.instruction: Step 5: Demonstrate fixing permissions — create a test file, restrict it, then fix access with chmod and chown.
steps.command:
touch /tmp/permfix.txt && chmod 600 /tmp/permfix.txt && echo 'Before:' && ls -l /tmp/permfix.txt
chmod 644 /tmp/permfix.txt && echo 'After chmod 644:' && ls -l /tmp/permfix.txt
steps.expectedOutput:
Before:
-rw------- 1 student student 0 Oct 10 14:30 /tmp/permfix.txt
After chmod 644:
-rw-r--r-- 1 student student 0 Oct 10 14:30 /tmp/permfix.txt

steps.instruction: Step 6: Show the complete permission denied diagnostic checklist and clean up.
steps.command:
rm /tmp/permfix.txt
echo '=== Permission Denied Checklist ==='
echo '1. ls -la <file>        → check file permissions and ownership'
echo '2. id                   → check your UID and groups'
echo '3. stat <file>          → see numeric mode (e.g., 0640)'
echo '4. namei -l <path>      → check ALL parent directory permissions'
echo '5. Fix options:'
echo '   chmod g+r <file>     → add group read permission'
echo '   chown :mygroup <file> → change group ownership'
echo '   usermod -aG group user → add user to the file group'
echo '   (user must re-login for group changes to take effect)'
steps.expectedOutput:
=== Permission Denied Checklist ===
1. ls -la <file>        → check file permissions and ownership
2. id                   → check your UID and groups
3. stat <file>          → see numeric mode (e.g., 0640)
4. namei -l <path>      → check ALL parent directory permissions
5. Fix options:
   chmod g+r <file>     → add group read permission
   chown :mygroup <file> → change group ownership
   usermod -aG group user → add user to the file group
   (user must re-login for group changes to take effect)
